heroOverview: Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (EIB) is narrowing of the airways that occurs during or within minutes after physical activity, driven by the inhalation of large volumes of dry, cold, or polluted air that strips moisture and heat from the airway lining. EIB affects roughly 10% of the general population and up to 90% of people who already have chronic asthma, making it the most common exercise-related respiratory condition worldwide. The defining symptom is breathlessness that comes on two to ten minutes after peak exertion and typically resolves within 30-60 minutes. Without recognition and management, EIB leads athletes to reduce or abandon physical activity, worsening overall fitness. Two puffs of a short-acting beta-agonist before exercise prevent the bronchoconstriction in most cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 10% of the general population; 40-90% of people with chronic asthma experience EIB during exercise
keyStats.demographics: Most commonly identified in children and adolescents; also prevalent in competitive athletes (estimates of 3-20% depending on the sport)
keyStats.avgAge: Can occur at any age; peak diagnosis in school-age children and adolescents aged 6-18
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 300 million people with asthma globally, the majority at risk of EIB during aerobic activity

definition: Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (ICD-10: J45.990) is transient narrowing of the lower airways provoked by vigorous physical exertion, distinct from but closely related to persistent asthma. During exercise, minute ventilation rises sharply, and the airways must condition far more air than at rest; the resulting heat and water loss from the bronchial mucosa activates inflammatory mediators — including mast cell-derived prostaglandins, leukotrienes, and histamine — that cause bronchial smooth muscle contraction. Peak airflow obstruction typically occurs 5-10 minutes after exercise stops and resolves spontaneously within 60 minutes as the airway warms and rehydrates. EIB is classified as occurring with or without underlying chronic asthma. When it occurs in isolation, it is termed EIB without asthma (EIBwa); when it superimposes on pre-existing asthma (ICD-10: J45.x), it is the most common asthma trigger in active individuals. The condition falls under the specialty of sports medicine and pulmonology. Population-level data suggest EIB affects at least 10% of the general population, with rates of 50-90% among people with known asthma and 3-20% in competitive athletes depending on sport type and training environment.

typesClassification.description: Bronchoconstriction that occurs only with exercise in individuals who have no resting airway obstruction, normal spirometry between episodes, and no chronic asthma diagnosis. It is common in elite endurance athletes.

Bronchoconstriction triggered by exercise in someone with pre-existing chronic asthma; exercise is the most common asthma trigger, and this form accounts for the majority of EIB cases seen in general practice.

After an initial EIB episode, roughly 50% of individuals enter a one- to three-hour refractory period during which a second bout of equal exercise causes less bronchoconstriction, a feature used therapeutically in warm-up protocols.

A minority of patients develop a second wave of airway narrowing four to eight hours after exercise, thought to be driven by eosinophil influx; this is less common and more severe in people with atopy.

primarySymptoms: Shortness of breath during or within 5-10 minutes after stopping vigorous exercise — the cardinal symptom — which feels disproportionate to the intensity of exertion and distinguishes EIB from normal exercise fatigue.
Wheezing: a high-pitched, musical sound on exhalation caused by turbulent airflow through narrowed bronchi, often audible to the person themselves and sometimes to bystanders.
Chest tightness described as a band squeezing around the chest, typically starting near peak exertion and persisting for 20-30 minutes after stopping activity.
A persistent, dry or minimally productive cough that begins during exercise or immediately after and can last for 30-60 minutes; in children, cough may be the only symptom.
Unusual fatigue during exercise that seems out of proportion to fitness level, often leading athletes to reduce their pace or stop entirely before planned workout completion.
Prolonged recovery time after exercise, with the heart rate and breathing returning to normal more slowly than expected, reflecting sustained airway obstruction beyond the exercise period.

Throat tightness or a sensation of difficulty breathing in the upper chest; some patients localise symptoms to the throat rather than the lungs, occasionally causing confusion with vocal cord dysfunction.

Reduced exercise tolerance over time: athletes with undiagnosed EIB often train less intensively than peers, attributing progressive fitness losses to poor conditioning rather than a treatable airway problem.
earlyWarningSigns: A slight dry cough that starts within the first few minutes of vigorous aerobic activity.
Mild chest tightness that comes on during the last few minutes of a run or swim and fades quickly at rest.
Needing to slow down or stop during workouts that were previously manageable without difficulty.
Noticing that cold-weather exercise causes more breathing difficulty than the same exertion in warm, humid conditions.
emergencySigns: Severe breathlessness at rest that does not improve within 15-20 minutes after stopping exercise, or that worsens despite using a reliever inhaler.
Silent chest on examination — the absence of wheeze because airflow is too restricted — combined with increasing respiratory distress.
Cyanosis (blue or grey coloration around the lips or fingertips) indicating dangerously low blood oxygen.
Confusion, inability to complete sentences, or collapse, which signal severe respiratory failure requiring emergency services.
Failure to respond to two doses of a short-acting beta-agonist given five minutes apart.

causes.cause: Airway heat and water loss during hyperpnoea
causes.description: During exercise, ventilation increases up to 20-fold. Breathing through the mouth bypasses nasal warming and humidification, delivering cooler, drier air to the bronchi. The resulting osmotic shift causes mast cell degranulation and release of prostaglandins and leukotrienes, which constrict bronchial smooth muscle within minutes.

The bronchoconstricting effect of exercise is amplified in cold, dry air because the thermal and osmotic stress on the airway lining is greater. This explains why cross-country skiers, ice hockey players, and winter runners have among the highest rates of EIB across all sports.

Indoor swimming pools expose airways to chloramines — compounds formed when chlorine reacts with nitrogen in sweat and urine. Repeated inhalation damages the epithelial barrier and heightens airway reactivity, contributing to the high prevalence of EIB among competitive swimmers.

Pre-existing eosinophilic or allergic airway inflammation lowers the threshold for exercise-triggered bronchoconstriction. People with allergic asthma or allergic rhinitis already have sensitised mast cells and a primed inflammatory cascade, making exercise a potent additional trigger.

Viral respiratory infections — particularly rhinovirus and respiratory syncytial virus in childhood — cause lasting increases in airway reactivity, and episodes of post-viral bronchoconstriction can persist for weeks. Repeated infections in atopic individuals may establish persistent EIB.

Years of training at high ventilatory loads — as in elite cyclists, marathon runners, and rowers — cause cumulative epithelial stress that can induce airway remodelling and de novo EIB even in athletes with no prior asthma history, a form sometimes called training-induced airway dysfunction.

riskFactors.description: The strongest single risk factor: 40-90% of people with chronic asthma experience EIB, because pre-existing airway inflammation and hyperresponsiveness prime the bronchi to respond to exercise-related triggers.

Atopic individuals — those with allergic rhinitis, eczema, or food allergy — have elevated IgE and sensitised mast cells systemically, increasing EIB risk approximately twofold compared with non-atopic peers.

Sports performed in cold dry air (ice hockey, cross-country skiing, speed skating) carry EIB prevalence estimates of 15-50%, compared with 3-10% in warm-weather sports, directly reflecting the airway-cooling mechanism.
Chloramine exposure in indoor pools is associated with airway epithelial damage; elite swimmers show EIB rates of 30-46% in screening studies, higher than most dry-land sports.

Excess body weight mechanically reduces functional residual capacity and increases the airway-cooling demand per breath; obesity is associated with an approximately 1.5-2× increased EIB risk and complicates spirometric interpretation.

Women show somewhat higher rates of EIB than men in population studies, a difference thought to reflect smaller absolute airway dimensions relative to lung volume and possible hormonal effects on mast cell activity.

Sedentary individuals who begin vigorous exercise have less-conditioned airways and higher ventilatory demands per unit of exertion; progressive aerobic conditioning reduces the per-session ventilatory load and may decrease EIB severity over time.
Exercising in areas with high particulate matter (PM2.5) or ozone concentrations magnifies airway oxidative stress and inflammation, increasing both the frequency and severity of EIB episodes.

affectedDemographics: Children and adolescents aged 6-18, in whom EIB is the leading cause of unexplained exercise intolerance
Competitive endurance athletes (runners, cyclists, swimmers, cross-country skiers) across all age groups
People with chronic asthma, among whom EIB during physical activity is nearly universal
Individuals with allergic rhinitis or other atopic conditions
Winter sports participants exposed to sustained cold-air breathing
Military recruits undergoing high-intensity conditioning in varied climates
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing EIB begins with a structured clinical history that establishes whether symptoms consistently arise during or within 10-15 minutes of stopping vigorous exercise and resolve spontaneously within 60 minutes. A history of asthma, atopy, or allergic rhinitis substantially increases pre-test probability. Resting spirometry is often normal in EIB without underlying asthma, which is why a normal spirogram does not exclude the diagnosis. Objective confirmation requires a bronchoprovocation challenge. The American Thoracic Society guideline recommends a standardised exercise challenge test (ECT) on a treadmill or cycle ergometer, targeting 85-90% of predicted maximum heart rate for six to eight minutes while monitoring forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1); a fall of ≥10% from baseline confirms EIB. Where laboratory exercise testing is unavailable, an Eucapnic Voluntary Hyperpnoea (EVH) challenge — breathing dry air at high ventilatory rates without exercise — serves as a surrogate and is the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA)-preferred test for elite athletes. Methacholine challenge testing can detect airway hyperresponsiveness but is less specific for EIB than EVH. A practical field approach — documenting ≥10% FEV1 fall after a measured outdoor or gym workout — is accepted in community settings. Fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) above 25 ppb supports eosinophilic airway inflammation and helps guide inhaled corticosteroid use but does not replace the challenge test. Key differentials to exclude include vocal cord dysfunction, cardiac arrhythmia, deconditioning, and hyperventilation syndrome, because each can mimic EIB in athletes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objective confirmation of EIB by measuring the fall in FEV1 after a defined exercise bout; a ≥10% FEV1 drop is the diagnostic threshold per ATS guidelines.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You run on a treadmill or cycle for six to eight minutes at 85-90% of your predicted maximum heart rate while breathing dry air; spirometry is performed before exercise and at 5, 10, 15, 20, and 30 minutes after stopping. Bronchodilator is given to reverse any drop. The test takes about 90 minutes in total.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The WADA-approved surrogate for exercise challenge, preferred when mimicking the high ventilatory conditions of elite training; higher sensitivity than standard ECT for EIB.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You breathe a dry air mixture containing 5% CO2 at 85% of your maximum voluntary ventilation for six minutes through a mouthpiece; spirometry is repeated every few minutes after. The test can trigger bronchospasm, so it is always performed with a bronchodilator immediately available.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses baseline airway function and rules out fixed obstruction (COPD), restriction, or normal airway physiology; resting values are often entirely normal in EIB without asthma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You breathe as hard and fast as you can into a mouthpiece connected to a flow sensor, repeated three times for reproducibility, typically in under 20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures eosinophilic airway inflammation; values above 25 ppb suggest a steroid-responsive inflammatory component and guide whether to add an inhaled corticosteroid.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You exhale steadily through a handheld device at a controlled flow for about ten seconds; results appear immediately on a screen. No preparation or bronchodilator is needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects non-specific airway hyperresponsiveness when diagnosis remains uncertain; it is more sensitive but less specific for EIB than EVH.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You inhale progressively higher concentrations of methacholine through a nebuliser; spirometry is checked after each dose. If FEV1 falls ≥20%, the test is positive and a bronchodilator is given. The session takes 60-90 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A low-cost practical method to document exercise-related airflow variability in community settings when formal challenge testing is unavailable.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You record peak expiratory flow readings before, immediately after, and 10 minutes after a standardised bout of your usual sport; a ≥15% drop from pre-exercise baseline is a practical positive threshold.

treatmentOverview: Management of EIB follows a stepwise approach endorsed by the ATS clinical practice guideline (2013) and the Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) report. The foundation is identifying and minimising triggering conditions — cold air, high pollution, dry environments — and using a structured warm-up. Pharmacological treatment is stratified by frequency and severity. For intermittent EIB, a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) such as albuterol (salbutamol) or levalbuterol inhaled 15-30 minutes before exercise prevents bronchoconstriction in approximately 80-95% of cases and remains the most widely recommended first-line prophylactic agent. Daily use should be avoided because regular SABA use more than four days per week leads to tolerance, reducing prophylactic efficacy by roughly 50%. When EIB occurs alongside poorly controlled chronic asthma, optimising controller therapy — typically an inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) — reduces baseline airway inflammation and directly attenuates EIB. For EIB persisting despite SABA pretreatment, GINA guidelines support adding a long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) only as an adjunct to ICS, never as monotherapy. Leukotriene receptor antagonists (LTRAs) such as montelukast attenuate EIB independently of SABA tolerance and are particularly useful in athletes who also have allergic rhinitis. Inhaled cromoglicate provides modest prophylaxis in children and where SABAs are not tolerated. Non-pharmacological measures — a 10-15 minute progressive warm-up exploiting the refractory period, wearing a heat-exchange mask in cold weather, and nasal breathing where possible — reduce episode frequency and should be part of every management plan.

medicalTreatments.name: Short-acting beta-agonists (SABA) — albuterol/salbutamol
medicalTreatments.description: Inhaled 15-30 minutes before exercise, SABAs cause bronchial smooth muscle relaxation by activating beta-2 adrenergic receptors, preventing the exercise-triggered narrowing. Two puffs (200 mcg) via a metered-dose inhaler is the standard prophylactic dose. Duration of protection is 2-4 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevent EIB in approximately 80-95% of episodes when used correctly before exertion; tolerance develops with use more than 4 days per week, reducing efficacy by roughly 50% with daily use.

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS)
medicalTreatments.description: Agents such as budesonide, fluticasone, or beclomethasone taken daily reduce underlying eosinophilic airway inflammation, lowering the threshold at which exercise triggers bronchoconstriction. They are the cornerstone controller therapy for any patient whose EIB occurs in the context of persistent asthma. Effect on EIB takes days to weeks to become apparent.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Regular ICS use reduces EIB severity and the magnitude of FEV1 fall by 30-50% compared with placebo in controlled trials in people with asthma-associated EIB.

medicalTreatments.name: Leukotriene receptor antagonists (LTRA) — montelukast
medicalTreatments.description: Oral montelukast (10 mg/day in adults, 5 mg/day in children 6-14 years) blocks leukotriene D4, a potent mediator of exercise-triggered bronchoconstriction. Unlike SABAs, tolerance does not develop with daily use, making LTRAs a useful alternative or add-on for frequent exercisers. A single dose two hours before exercise also has prophylactic effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Montelukast reduces exercise-induced FEV1 fall by approximately 45-50% compared with placebo and is similarly effective to SABAs in multiple randomised trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Long-acting beta-agonists (LABA) — formoterol, salmeterol
medicalTreatments.description: Added to ICS — never used alone — for athletes with frequent EIB not controlled by SABA alone. Formoterol has a rapid onset and may be used as both controller and reliever in ICS/formoterol combination inhalers. WADA requires a Therapeutic Use Exemption (TUE) for LABAs above specified inhaled thresholds in competitive sport.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ICS/LABA combination reduces EIB frequency by approximately 60-70% compared with ICS alone in studies of athletes with moderate-to-severe asthma.

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled cromoglicate or nedocromil
medicalTreatments.description: Mast cell stabilisers that attenuate the initial mediator release triggered by airway dehydration during exercise. Given 15-20 minutes before exertion, they provide moderate prophylaxis particularly in children and in individuals who cannot use beta-agonists. They are not subject to WADA restrictions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce the post-exercise FEV1 fall by approximately 40-60% compared with placebo in short-term challenge studies; less efficacious overall than SABA pretreatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Progressive warm-up protocol exploiting the refractory period
alternativeTreatments.description: A structured 10-15 minute warm-up at 50-60% of maximum effort, followed by several short high-intensity intervals, triggers a mild EIB episode that induces the refractory period. The subsequent main activity then causes little or no bronchoconstriction. Studies show this non-pharmacological strategy reduces the exercise-induced FEV1 fall by 50% or more in susceptible athletes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cold-air face mask / heat-moisture exchanger
alternativeTreatments.description: Wearing a light filter mask or scarf over the mouth and nose during cold-weather exercise reconditions inhaled air, reducing thermal and osmotic airway stress. Small studies show reduced EIB severity during winter outdoor exercise, with no pharmacological side effects.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nasal breathing training
alternativeTreatments.description: Breathing through the nose rather than the mouth warms and humidifies inspired air before it reaches the bronchi. Nasal breathing is difficult at intensities above roughly 70% VO2 max, but low-to-moderate exercise with nasal breathing demonstrably attenuates EIB compared with mouth breathing in controlled experiments.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin C supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: A 2014 Cochrane review of eight randomised trials found that 0.5-1 g of ascorbic acid taken before exercise reduced post-exercise FEV1 fall by approximately 48% on average, potentially via antioxidant attenuation of airway oxidative stress; evidence quality is moderate and it is not yet a guideline-endorsed standalone strategy.

whySeeSpecialist: A sports medicine physician or pulmonologist should be consulted when EIB symptoms are not fully controlled by pre-exercise bronchodilator use, when symptoms occur at rest, when an objective diagnosis is needed for a competitive athlete requiring WADA documentation, or when the pattern of breathlessness does not fit classic EIB (raising concern for cardiac, vocal cord, or metabolic causes). Sports medicine specialists can perform or arrange EVH challenge testing, interpret WADA therapeutic use exemption requirements, and tailor management to training demands.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a sports medicine physician or a pulmonologist with experience in exercise-related respiratory conditions and, for competitive athletes, familiarity with WADA anti-doping regulations. Access to a laboratory capable of standardised exercise or EVH challenge testing is important for objective diagnosis. For children, a paediatric pulmonologist is preferable. If allergic rhinitis co-exists, a clinician comfortable prescribing combined nasal corticosteroid and LTRA therapy will achieve better overall airway control.
preventionStrategies: Use a pre-exercise SABA 15-30 minutes before vigorous activity on days when full exertion is expected, but limit this to fewer than four days per week to preserve efficacy.
Perform a 10-15 minute progressive warm-up before high-intensity effort to exploit the refractory period and blunt the main bronchoconstriction response.
Monitor ambient air quality and temperature; avoid peak outdoor exercise on days with high pollution, low temperatures below -10°C, or high pollen counts.
Optimise chronic asthma controller therapy — inadequately treated background inflammation greatly amplifies exercise-triggered episodes.
Use nasal breathing techniques during low-to-moderate intensity sessions to warm and humidify inspired air.
Wear a heat-moisture-exchange mask during unavoidable cold-weather exercise to reduce the thermal airway stress that drives EIB.
lifestyleModifications: Choose exercise environments with warm, humid air — swimming in an outdoor or well-ventilated pool, indoor cycling, or walking in temperate weather — over cold outdoor sports when symptoms are poorly controlled.
Taper exercise intensity gradually rather than stopping abruptly; a five-minute cool-down reduces the rapid airway temperature changes that can trigger post-exercise bronchoconstriction.
Always carry a reliever inhaler during physical activity, even on symptom-free days, and know how to recognise when symptoms require stopping.
Track symptom patterns with a training diary noting temperature, humidity, pollution, and pre-exercise medication use to identify personal triggers.
Maintain regular moderate aerobic conditioning, which reduces the ventilatory demand per unit of exertion over time and may attenuate baseline airway hyperresponsiveness.
Treat allergic rhinitis aggressively with nasal corticosteroids and antihistamines, since upper airway inflammation amplifies lower airway sensitivity to exercise.

recommended: A Mediterranean-pattern diet rich in vegetables, fruit, fish, and olive oil, associated with lower asthma prevalence and airway inflammation in epidemiological studies
Foods high in antioxidants — berries, leafy greens, and citrus — which may reduce oxidative stress in the airway epithelium triggered by high-ventilation exercise
Magnesium-containing foods such as nuts, seeds, and legumes; magnesium acts as a natural bronchodilator and low dietary intake is associated with greater airway hyperresponsiveness
Adequate hydration before and during exercise to maintain airway surface liquid and reduce mucosal dehydration during hyperpnoea
avoid: Highly processed and refined-sugar foods, associated in prospective studies with higher asthma incidence and potentially increased airway inflammatory responses
Sulphite-containing foods and beverages — dried fruit, wine, some processed meats — which can trigger airway narrowing in approximately 5-10% of people with asthma
Heavy meals within 90 minutes before exercise, as gastric distension can restrict diaphragm movement and worsen dyspnoea during exertion
Excessive caffeine on exercise days without medical guidance, since caffeine has mild bronchodilator properties but can also increase heart rate and complicate symptom interpretation

exerciseGuidelines: Regular physical activity is strongly encouraged in EIB and should not be curtailed beyond what is needed for safety. Well-managed EIB should allow full participation in sport at all levels. Warm-water swimming is consistently the most EIB-friendly aerobic activity because inspired air is warm and humid. Walking, yoga, and cycling in temperate environments are also well tolerated. High-intensity cold-air endurance sports carry the highest risk and require careful pharmacological preparation. Every session should begin with a progressive warm-up and end with a five-minute cool-down. Competitive athletes should confirm their medication regimen is compliant with applicable anti-doping regulations before competition.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed controller therapy (ICS, LTRA) consistently every day, not only on exercise days — background inflammation determines how reactive the airways are.
Pre-medicate with a SABA 15-30 minutes before planned vigorous sessions on days when this is indicated by your care plan, but track how many days per week you need to use it.
Check the weather and air quality index each morning; adjust session intensity or timing if outdoor conditions are poor.
Keep a symptom and peak flow log to spot deteriorating trends before they become acute episodes.
Review your management plan with your clinician at least annually, or sooner if you need your reliever inhaler more than four times a week.
prognosis: The prognosis for EIB with appropriate management is excellent. The vast majority of affected individuals — including elite athletes — can participate fully in sport when the condition is identified and treated. In children, EIB often improves with age as absolute airway dimensions increase relative to body mass, and some outgrow the problem entirely. EIB without underlying asthma tends to be stable and medication-responsive. In people with concurrent asthma, long-term prognosis depends primarily on how well the underlying asthma is controlled; uncontrolled asthma greatly amplifies EIB severity. Training in harsh environments over many years may gradually increase airway reactivity in elite endurance athletes, but this is reversible with cessation of high-level training in most cases.
recoveryTimeline: An acute EIB episode following exercise typically peaks 5-10 minutes after exertion stops and resolves spontaneously within 30-60 minutes as the airway warms and rehydrates. A pre-exercise SABA shortens and blunts the episode substantially. A late-phase response, if it occurs, begins 4-8 hours after exercise. With regular treatment, most patients notice meaningful reduction in symptom frequency and severity within two to four weeks of starting a controller medication such as an ICS or LTRA. Full optimisation of an exercise training programme alongside medical therapy typically takes 6-12 weeks.
complications: Severe acute bronchoconstriction during exercise, which in rare cases may trigger a near-fatal asthma attack if a reliever inhaler is unavailable and exposure to the trigger continues, particularly in cold-air sports.

Chronic exercise avoidance: poorly managed or undiagnosed EIB leads many patients — particularly children — to reduce or abandon physical activity, contributing to obesity, cardiovascular deconditioning, and reduced quality of life.

Underperformance and misdiagnosis in athletes: EIB is frequently attributed to poor fitness rather than a treatable condition, delaying diagnosis by months to years and causing unnecessary loss of athletic development.

Remodelling with chronic severe asthma: in individuals with very poorly controlled asthma-associated EIB, repeated cycles of inflammation and repair can contribute to fixed airway remodelling, reducing the reversibility of obstruction over time.

Anti-doping rule violations: competitive athletes who use prohibited beta-agonists above allowed inhaled thresholds without a valid WADA Therapeutic Use Exemption face sanctions; proper documentation protects athletes with genuine EIB.

supportResources.name: NHLBI — Asthma and Exercise
supportResources.description: Patient-oriented guidance from the U.S. National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute on managing asthma during physical activity.

supportResources.name: Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Free patient guide from the GINA group covering asthma management including exercise-related triggers, updated annually.

supportResources.name: Asthma and Allergy Foundation of America — Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction
supportResources.description: Accessible condition overview with practical tips for athletes and parents of children with EIB from a major U.S. advocacy organization.

sources.title: Parsons JP et al. An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Exercise-Induced Bronchoconstriction. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2013;187(9):1016-1027
Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA). Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention. 2024 Report.
NHLBI — Asthma Clinical Guidelines and Resources

confusedWithConditions.name: Vocal cord dysfunction
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Vocal cord dysfunction causes inspiratory stridor and throat tightness that peak during exercise and resolve almost immediately on stopping, whereas EIB causes expiratory wheeze that peaks 5-10 minutes after exercise ends.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic asthma at rest
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic asthma causes symptoms at rest and overnight as well as with exertion; EIB without asthma is purely exercise-triggered with a normal resting spirometry between episodes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: COPD causes fixed airflow obstruction that worsens steadily over years and affects smokers over 40, whereas EIB is reversible, often occurs in young athletes, and shows normal spirometry at rest.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyperventilation syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyperventilation during exercise causes tingling, dizziness, and chest tightness without true airflow obstruction on spirometry, whereas EIB produces a measurable ≥10% FEV1 fall on challenge testing.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic asthma and EIB share the same underlying airway hyperresponsiveness; EIB is present in the majority of people with asthma and its management is central to asthma care.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Atopic sensitisation amplifies exercise-triggered mast cell activation, making allergic asthma the form of asthma most strongly associated with frequent and severe EIB.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic rhinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Upper airway allergic inflammation spills into the lower airway and increases bronchial hyperresponsiveness; treating rhinitis concurrently often reduces EIB severity.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
relatedConditions.relevance: COPD causes fixed exercise limitation that can coexist with or mimic EIB in adults over 40; distinguishing the two requires spirometry and a bronchoprovocation challenge.

faqs.question: What is the difference between exercise-induced asthma and regular asthma?
faqs.answer: Regular (chronic) asthma causes airway narrowing at rest, overnight, and with many triggers including allergens and cold air. Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (EIB) is triggered specifically by vigorous physical activity and resolves within 30-60 minutes of stopping. EIB can occur in someone with no asthma at rest. When both are present, exercise is the most common asthma trigger.

faqs.question: How do I know if my breathlessness during exercise is EIB or just poor fitness?
faqs.answer: EIB produces audible wheeze or chest tightness that appears 5-10 minutes after stopping exercise and takes 20-60 minutes to fully resolve — fitness-related breathlessness stops quickly once you slow down. A formal diagnosis requires spirometry before and after a structured exercise or EVH challenge test confirming a ≥10% fall in FEV1. If you consistently struggle to breathe after exercise stops, a clinician evaluation is warranted.

faqs.question: Can I exercise with exercise-induced asthma?
faqs.answer: Yes, most people with EIB can exercise fully, including at elite levels. Proper treatment — a pre-exercise bronchodilator, a warm-up protocol, and controller therapy if needed — allows full participation in sport. Many Olympic athletes have EIB. The goal of management is to eliminate symptoms, not reduce activity.

faqs.question: Which inhaler do I use before exercise for EIB?
faqs.answer: Two puffs of a short-acting beta-agonist (albuterol/salbutamol, 200 mcg) inhaled 15-30 minutes before vigorous exercise prevents EIB in about 80-95% of cases. Use this pre-exercise dose only on days of planned vigorous activity and no more than four days per week; more frequent use leads to tolerance and reduced effectiveness.

faqs.question: Does EIB go away on its own as children get older?
faqs.answer: Many children with EIB improve as they grow because airway calibre increases relative to body size and ventilatory demand per session. Some outgrow the condition entirely in adolescence or early adulthood. In children with underlying atopic asthma, however, the asthma persists and EIB control depends on managing the background condition. Annual review by a clinician is recommended.

faqs.question: What sports are best for people with EIB?
faqs.answer: Warm, humid-air activities cause the least EIB: swimming in outdoor or well-ventilated pools, walking, golf, and yoga are well tolerated. Team sports with intermittent effort (basketball, baseball) are better tolerated than sustained aerobic endurance sports. Cold-air sports such as cross-country skiing and ice hockey carry the highest risk, but can be managed with medication and warm-up protocols.

faqs.question: Is a pre-exercise inhaler banned in sport?
faqs.answer: Inhaled albuterol/salbutamol and formoterol are permitted by WADA up to defined daily inhaled doses without a Therapeutic Use Exemption. Amounts above those thresholds — or any systemic beta-agonist — require a TUE. Competitive athletes should check WADA's Prohibited List for the current year and consult a sports medicine doctor to ensure their EIB medication is compliant.

faqs.question: How is EIB diagnosed in children who can't do a full spirometry test?
faqs.answer: Young children under five often cannot perform reliable spirometry. Clinicians use clinical history, response to a pre-exercise bronchodilator trial, impulse oscillometry, or a supervised field exercise test with peak flow monitoring instead. A clear pattern of symptoms consistently triggered by activity and relieved by a bronchodilator is sufficient for a working diagnosis and treatment trial in young children.

faqs.question: Why does swimming cause less EIB than running in cold air?
faqs.answer: Swimming inspires warm, humid air just above the pool surface, minimising the airway heat and water loss that drives bronchoconstriction. Cold outdoor running delivers dry, cold air that must be conditioned by the airways during high-ventilation exercise, maximising osmotic and thermal stress on the bronchial lining — the core mechanism of EIB.

faqs.question: What is the refractory period in EIB?
faqs.answer: After a bout of exercise that provokes EIB, about 50% of people enter a refractory period lasting 1-3 hours during which a second exercise session at the same intensity causes little or no bronchoconstriction. A structured warm-up that deliberately triggers a mild episode before main training exploits this refractoriness to protect airways during the main workout.

faqs.question: Does EIB cause permanent lung damage?
faqs.answer: EIB in isolation does not cause permanent lung damage in most people. If it occurs in the context of severe, chronically uncontrolled asthma with repeated inflammation, airway remodelling can develop over years, reducing the reversibility of obstruction. Treating EIB and its underlying asthma effectively prevents this progression.

faqs.question: Can adults develop EIB for the first time?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults with no prior asthma history can develop EIB, particularly elite endurance athletes after years of high-ventilation training, or following respiratory infections that increase airway reactivity. Onset after age 40 should also prompt investigation to rule out fixed obstruction from COPD or cardiac causes of exertional dyspnoea.

faqs.question: What is an EVH challenge test and when is it used?
faqs.answer: Eucapnic Voluntary Hyperpnoea (EVH) involves breathing dry air at high flow rates for six minutes without exercise, mimicking the ventilatory demand of intense training. It is the WADA-preferred test for elite athletes seeking a Therapeutic Use Exemption for beta-agonists. EVH has higher sensitivity for EIB than a standard exercise test in a laboratory setting.

faqs.question: Will I need to take medication before every exercise session?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Many people with mild EIB can manage with non-pharmacological measures — a proper warm-up, choosing warm-humid environments, and nasal breathing — and only need a pre-exercise inhaler before high-intensity or cold-air sessions. People with more frequent or severe EIB, or with underlying asthma, typically benefit from daily controller therapy that reduces the need for pre-exercise bronchodilator use.

faqs.question: How long does an EIB episode last without treatment?
faqs.answer: An untreated EIB episode typically peaks 5-15 minutes after stopping exercise and resolves on its own within 30-60 minutes as the airways warm and rehydrate. A short-acting bronchodilator accelerates recovery to within minutes. A late-phase response, if it occurs, can appear 4-8 hours after exercise and lasts 1-3 hours.

faqs.question: Is montelukast effective for EIB?
faqs.answer: Montelukast (a leukotriene receptor antagonist) reduces the post-exercise FEV1 fall by approximately 45-50% compared with placebo in randomised trials. Unlike inhaled SABAs, tolerance does not develop with daily use, making it useful for people who exercise daily. It is particularly beneficial when allergic rhinitis coexists. It takes 1-2 hours to reach peak effect so is not a rescue medication.

faqs.question: Is EIB more common in women or men?
faqs.answer: Epidemiological studies consistently show slightly higher rates of EIB in women than men, possibly because women have smaller absolute airway dimensions relative to lung volume and because mast cell activity may be influenced by sex hormones. The clinical presentation and treatment approach are the same regardless of sex.

faqs.question: Can I develop EIB from exercising in a swimming pool?
faqs.answer: Competitive swimmers training intensively in indoor chlorinated pools have some of the highest EIB rates among athletes — around 30-46% in elite screening studies. Chloramine vapours generated by the reaction of chlorine with body fluids damage the airway epithelium over years of exposure, increasing reactivity. Outdoor pools and well-ventilated facilities carry lower risk.

faqs.question: How is EIB treated in someone who also has chronic asthma?
faqs.answer: When EIB occurs with chronic asthma, optimising daily controller therapy — typically an inhaled corticosteroid, sometimes with a leukotriene receptor antagonist — is the priority, since reducing background airway inflammation substantially attenuates exercise-triggered episodes. A pre-exercise SABA and warm-up protocol are added on top. GINA guidelines guide the step-up approach based on symptom frequency and FEV1.

faqs.question: Does vitamin C prevent EIB?
faqs.answer: A Cochrane review of eight randomised trials found vitamin C (0.5-1 g before exercise) reduced post-exercise FEV1 fall by about 48% on average. Evidence is moderate and it is not a guideline-endorsed standalone treatment, but it is safe as a low-cost adjunct.

faqs.question: Should I see a specialist or can my GP manage EIB?
faqs.answer: A GP can diagnose and manage straightforward EIB with a pre-exercise SABA and basic lifestyle advice. Referral to a sports medicine physician or pulmonologist is appropriate if symptoms persist despite first-line treatment, if objective challenge testing is needed for competitive sport clearance or a WADA TUE, or if the diagnosis is uncertain.

metaDescription: Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction causes wheezing and chest tightness in sport. Learn symptoms, EVH diagnosis, and treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
